title: `--x-initial-edge` behavior change in Verilator 5.x
body:
How may we help - what is your question?
# Issue Description

In Verilator 5.x, the `--x-initial-edge` option triggers both posedge and negedge events on the first `eval()` call, regardless of the actual signal transition (X→0 or X→1). This causes unwanted edge triggers that can break test assertions and verification code.

## Problem

- **Expected behavior**: X→0 should only trigger negedge, X→1 should only trigger posedge
- **Actual behavior in v5.x**: Both negedge AND posedge are triggered on first eval(), regardless of transition direction
- **Impact**: Test code with edge-sensitive assertions fails unexpectedly

### Test Case
```verilog
module reset_test (input logic clk);
   always_ff @ (posedge clk) begin
        $display("posedge detected!");
   end

   always_ff @ (negedge clk) begin
        $display("negedge detected!");
   end
endmodule
```
### Test Results
**Verilator 4.x with `--x-initial-edge`:**
```
posedge detected!
```
or
```
negedge detected!
```

**Verilator 5.x with `--x-initial-edge`:**
```
posedge detected!
negedge detected!
```

 I do not know whether this behavior change in newer Verilator versions was intentional or a bug.
code_blocks:
module reset_test (input logic clk);
   always_ff @ (posedge clk) begin
        $display("posedge detected!");
   end

   always_ff @ (negedge clk) begin
        $display("negedge detected!");
   end
endmodule
posedge detected!
negedge detected!
posedge detected!
negedge detected!
